description.en-US: Aquinas's commentary on Aristotle's *Posterior Analytics* — on demonstrative science, definitions, and the first principles of scientific knowledge.
description.la: Expositio *Posteriorum Analyticorum* Aristotelis — de demonstrativa scientia, definitionibus, et primis principiis cognitionis scientificae.

sources.description: English translation by Fabian R. Larcher OP (1970), revised by the Aquinas Institute. Mirrored from aquinas.cc.
Latin text from the Leonine / Marietti editions (public domain), mirrored from aquinas.cc.

title.en-US: Book 1 — The Demonstrative Syllogism
Lecture 1 — The Need for Preexistent Knowledge in All Learning
Lecture 2 — Extent and order of the preexistent knowledge required for obtaining science
Lecture 3 — Preexistent knowledge of the conclusion
Lecture 4 — Nature of the demonstrative syllogism
Lecture 5 — First and immediate propositions
Lecture 6 — Knowledge of immediate principles
Lecture 7 — Discussion of two errors—exclusion of the first one
Lecture 8 — The second error is excluded by showing that circular demonstration is not acceptable
Lecture 9 — How something is said to be predicated of all
Lecture 10 — How something is said to be predicated per se of a thing
Lecture 11 — How something is said to be predicated as commensurately universal
Lecture 12 — How error occurs in taking the universal
Lecture 13 — Demonstration proceeds from necessary things
Lecture 14 — Demonstration bears upon and proceeds from things which are per se
Lecture 15 — Demonstration does not skip from one genus to an alien genus
Lecture 16 — Demonstration is not of perishable but of eternal matters
Lecture 17 — Demonstration does not proceed from common principles, but from principles proper to the thing demonstrated
Lecture 18 — Difference between principles and non-principles, common and proper principles
Lecture 19 — How common principles differ from one another
Lecture 20 — Relation between demonstrative sciences and common principles
Lecture 21 — The questions, responses and disputations peculiar to each science
Lecture 22 — Each science has its own deceptions and areas of ignorance
Lecture 23 — How demonstrations quia and propter quid differ in the same science; demonstration quia through an effect
Lecture 24 — How there is demonstration quia through things not immediately connected
Lecture 25 — How demonstration quia differs from demonstration propter quid when the former pertains to one science and the latter to another
Lecture 26 — Demonstrative syllogisms best made in the first figure; on mediate and immediate negative propositions
Lecture 27 — How ignorance or deception bearing on first and immediate things can be induced by syllogism and lead one to suppose something to be which is not
Lecture 28 — How by syllogizing in the first or second figure a false negative is concluded contrary to an immediate affirmative
Lecture 29 — Syllogism ignorance in regard to mediate propositions
Lecture 30 — Cause of simple negative ignorance
Lecture 31 — Three questions about proceeding to infinity in confirming demonstrations
Lecture 32 — Solution of some of these doubts hinges upon solution of others
Lecture 33 — That one does not proceed to infinity in essential predicates is shown logically
Lecture 34 — Logical reasons why one does not proceed to infinity in predicates
Lecture 35 — That there is not an infinite process upward or downward in predicates is shown analytically
Lecture 36 — Certain corollaries from preceding lecture
Lecture 37 — Whether universal demonstration is stronger than particular demonstration
Lecture 38 — Universal demonstration is stronger than particular demonstration
Lecture 39 — Affirmative demonstration is stronger than negative
Lecture 40 — Negative ostensive demonstration is stronger than demonstration leading to the impossible
Lecture 41 — Comparison of science to science from standpoint of certainty and of unity and diversity
Lecture 42 — Science is not concerned with things caused by fortune or with things learned through sense-perception
Lecture 43 — Principles of all syllogisms are not the same
Lecture 44 — Science compared with other modes of knowing
Book 2 — The Principles of the Demonstrative Syllogism
Lecture 1 — Each of the four questions which pertain to science is one way or another a question of the middle
Lecture 2 — Whether the definition which signifies the quod quid of a thing can be demonstrated
Lecture 3 — Whether the quod quid signified by the definition can be demonstrated by taking convertible terms
Lecture 4 — Whether quod quid can be demonstrated by the method of division
Lecture 5 — Whether a quod quid can be demonstrated by taking that which is required for a quod quid
Lecture 6 — Whether quod quid can be shown by demonstration or definition
Lecture 7 — The two ways, logical and demonstrative, of manifesting quod quid
Lecture 8 — To attain quod quid through demonstration is not possible in all cases: relation of definition to demonstration
Lecture 9 — Propter quid can be manifested in four genera of causes
Lecture 10 — How something is demonstrated through a cause not simultaneous with its caused: how a cause not simultaneous with its effect is taken as middle in demonstrating
Lecture 11 — How a cause which is not simultaneous with its effect may be taken as a middle in demonstration

Lecture 12 — How in things that come to be reciprocally, a cause which is not simultaneous with the effect is taken as middle in a demonstration; how one demonstrates through cause differently, in things that occur always and in things that occur as a general rule
Lecture 13 — Characteristics which should be present in the items which constitute the definition signifying the essence of a thing
Lecture 14 — Dividing the genus to investigate which items should be put in a definition
Lecture 15 — Two errors are excluded: what is really required for constituting a definition according to the method of division
Lecture 16 — How to search for the definition of a thing by examining things similar to it and dissimilar
Lecture 17 — How to investigate the why in special problems; how certain problems agree as to propter quid, either because their middles have a kind of unity or are subordinated
Lecture 18 — Coexistence of cause and caused
Lecture 19 — Whether upon unity of cause follows unity of effect, and vice versa; how cause and effect follow upon one another
Lecture 20 — How the first principles of demonstration are known by us
